title: What the US can learn from Europe on prediction markets—and vice versa
gist: With Kalshi and Polymarket reaching ~$24 billion combined trading volume in April 2026 and facing US congressional insider-trading scrutiny, the author argues Congress should adopt Europe's 2018 ESMA approach: ban binary-option/event-contract sales to retail (who face structurally negative expected returns) while preserving institutional access for hedging. Conversely, the US legal definition of event contracts as derivatives is cleaner than Europe's, which has pushed several countries to require gambling licenses.

insights.claim: Prediction-market platforms Kalshi and Polymarket reached approximately $24 billion in combined global trading volume in April 2026 and are now in the crosshairs of US congressional investigations into alleged military and political insider trading.
insights.speaker: Todd Phillips
insights.so_what: Quantifies a fast-scaling sector facing binary regulatory risk—position sizing in/around these platforms must price a possible retail ban.
insights.quote: their combined global trading volumes reaching approximately $24 billion in April
insights.locator: Since they burst into the public consciousness

insights.claim: US lawmakers have introduced twenty-one bills to date addressing prediction markets, ranging from insider-trading bans to excluding sports/election contracts to gambling-like regulation.
insights.speaker: Todd Phillips
insights.so_what: A crowded, divergent bill landscape signals high regulatory uncertainty and headline risk for the sector through 2026.

insights.claim: The author argues Congress should follow Europe's lead and ban the sale of binary-outcome derivatives to retail traders while preserving institutional access, protecting retail and curbing insider profiteering simultaneously.
insights.speaker: Todd Phillips
insights.so_what: The most likely durable regulatory template—retail ban + institutional carve-out—would gut the consumer growth story while preserving B2B use.
insights.quote: banning the sale of derivatives with binary outcomes to retail traders.

insights.claim: ESMA's 2018 ban rested on binary options having a 'structural expected negative return' for retail investors, who lack the information and systems to price them and thus lose more cumulatively the more they trade.
insights.speaker: Todd Phillips
insights.so_what: The negative-EV framing is the regulatory hook most portable to the US—the core argument that could justify a retail ban.
insights.quote: the more positions an investor takes, the more likely they are to lose money on a cumulative basis.

insights.claim: Following ESMA's 2018 determination, virtually all EEA nations banned binary options for retail, leaving Switzerland as the only OECD nation in Europe still permitting retail binary-options trading.
insights.speaker: Todd Phillips
insights.so_what: Shows how comprehensively a retail ban can sweep a region once a lead regulator acts—precedent for rapid US contagion if the SEC/CFTC move.

insights.claim: The author argues the US definition of event contracts as derivatives is cleaner than Europe's, because EU law's 'financial instrument' test may not capture many Kalshi/Polymarket contracts, pushing countries like France, the UK and Spain to require gambling licenses or shutdowns.
insights.speaker: Todd Phillips
insights.so_what: US institutional hedging access may survive even a retail ban, whereas EU treatment is harsher—jurisdiction matters for where the institutional market concentrates.
insights.quote: It is unclear whether many of the event contracts listed by Kalshi or Polymarket would satisfy that definition.
insights.locator: Where Washington may be better equipped than Brussels

insights.claim: The author reframes the core regulatory question as not whether prediction markets should be permitted but who should have access—retail tends to treat event contracts as gambling while businesses can use them to hedge economic risk.
insights.speaker: Todd Phillips
insights.so_what: Frames the regulatory endgame as an access split—the variable that determines whether platforms keep their mass-retail business model.
insights.quote: the key question for lawmakers...is not simply whether prediction markets should be permitted, but rather who should have access to them.
